meta.description: Eichler model families used for visual identification. Individual plan numbers (e.g. AA-1, JE-101, OC-244) belong to a family; exact numbers come from tract site plans at the UC Berkeley Environmental Design Archives.
meta.note: Eras overlap — Eichler often built several families side by side in one tract.

families.name: Early Flat-Top
families.signature: Flat roof, simple rectangle, front door opens directly into the living space
families.description: The original Eichlers: compact 3-bedroom rectangles (roughly 1,000–1,300 sq ft) with flat roofs, radiant-heated concrete slab floors, mahogany paneling, a carport rather than a garage, and a glass window-wall facing the back yard. No atrium — the front facade is mostly solid with clerestory or slot windows.
howToSpot: Flat roof across the whole house
Front door opens directly into the living area (no atrium, no gallery hall)
Carport instead of an enclosed garage on many lots
Compact rectangular footprint

families.name: Low-Gable Classic
families.signature: Low-pitched gable over the living area, glass at the gable end
families.description: Mid-50s evolution: a low-slung gable roof rises over the main living space (often with a fully glazed gable end facing the yard) while bedroom wings stay flat-roofed. Homes grew to 4 bedrooms and added proper garages.
howToSpot: Low triangular gable over the living room, flat roof over bedroom wings
Floor-to-ceiling glass filling the gable end
Post-and-beam ceiling following the roof slope inside
Entry into a modest hall or multipurpose room, not an open-air atrium

families.name: Gallery Model
families.signature: Front door opens into a wide covered interior hallway (the "gallery")
families.description: Instead of an open-air atrium, the front door opens into a generous enclosed circulation spine — the gallery — that runs across the house and organizes the rooms around it. Often paired with flat or low-gable roofs.
howToSpot: Front door leads into a wide, fully roofed interior hall
Rooms branch off both sides of that hall
No open-to-the-sky space inside the house

families.name: Courtyard Model
families.signature: Entry through a walled front or side courtyard, house wraps partly around it
families.description: A transitional step toward the atrium: you pass through a gate into a private walled courtyard before reaching the front door. The courtyard sits at the front or side of the house rather than fully enclosed at its center.
howToSpot: Gate and walled outdoor courtyard before the front door
Courtyard is at the front/side edge of the house, not fully surrounded by rooms
L- or U-shaped floor plan wrapping the courtyard

families.name: Atrium Model
families.signature: Front door opens into an open-air atrium at the center of the house
families.description: The iconic Eichler. A mostly blank street facade hides a glass-walled, open-to-the-sky atrium at the heart of the house; every main room looks into it. Introduced in 1958 and built in huge numbers through the 60s and early 70s.
howToSpot: You open the front door and you're still outdoors — sky above you
Glass walls on all sides of that central court
Blank or nearly windowless street-facing facade
Rooms arranged in a ring around the atrium

families.name: Double A-Frame / High Gable
families.signature: One or two dramatic steep gables dominating the roofline
families.description: Claude Oakland's late-60s showpieces: steeply pitched A-frame gables (sometimes a pair) over the living space or atrium, giving soaring ceilings. Common in Upper Lucas Valley, Rancho Sans Souci, and other mid/late-60s tracts.
howToSpot: Steep, dramatic gable(s) visible from the street — much taller than the low-gable classics
Soaring tent-like ceiling in the living room
Usually combined with an atrium or gallery entry

families.name: Two-Story & Split-Level
families.signature: More than one level — rare for an Eichler
families.description: Uncommon: split-levels built on steep San Francisco hillsides (Diamond Heights), a two-story cluster in Terra Linda, and late two-story models in Palo Alto's Los Arboles Addition (1970–74).
howToSpot: Two full floors or a split-level section
Steep or urban lot
Post-and-beam ceilings and window-walls still present on the main level

quiz.question: Open your front door. What's on the other side?

options.label: An open-air courtyard — I can see the sky before entering the house
options.hint: Glass walls around it, rooms all the way around

options.label: A wide covered interior hallway
options.hint: Fully roofed, rooms branch off it

options.label: Directly into the living room / main space
options.hint: No separate entry space to speak of

options.label: I pass through a walled outdoor courtyard before the front door
options.hint: A gate, then a garden court, then the door

quiz.question: What does your roof look like from the street?

options.label: A low, gentle gable over part of the house
One or two dramatic, steep A-frame gables

quiz.question: How many levels does your home have?
